official_name: Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards
notes: OASIS is a non-profit international consortium that develops, converges, and adopts open standards. The Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) Technical Committee at OASIS maintains STIX and TAXII (transferred from MITRE around 2015). Other OASIS work includes the Common Security Advisory Framework (CSAF), OpenC2, and many non-security standards.

data.description: Structured language for representing cyber threat intelligence — threat actors, campaigns, malware, indicators, observed data, courses of action, vulnerabilities.
data.notes: STIX 2.1 is the current OASIS Standard (June 2021). Originally developed by MITRE under DHS sponsorship; transferred to OASIS Cyber Threat Intelligence Technical Committee. STIX 1.x (MITRE-era XML) is legacy. MITRE ATT&CK ships as STIX 2.0 bundles. Companion to TAXII.

examples.note: The STIX 2.1 specification as a whole

match_nodes.description: Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information

data.official_name: Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information
data.description: Application-layer protocol for the exchange of STIX cyber threat intelligence over HTTPS.
data.notes: TAXII 2.1 is the current OASIS Standard (June 2021). Defines servers, clients, channels, and collections for pulling/pushing STIX data. Originally MITRE/DHS; transferred to OASIS CTI TC. Companion to STIX.

examples.note: The TAXII 2.1 specification as a whole

data.description: Standardized JSON format for the distribution of machine-readable security advisories.
data.notes: CSAF 2.0 is the current OASIS Standard. Supersedes CVRF 1.x. Used by major vendors (Cisco, Microsoft, Red Hat, Oracle, etc.) for publishing security advisories. CISA encourages CSAF adoption.

examples.note: The CSAF 2.0 specification as a whole

data.description: JSON-based file format for the output of static analysis tools. Lets results from different SAST tools be aggregated, compared, and consumed uniformly.
data.notes: SARIF 2.1.0 is the current OASIS Standard (March 2020). Used by CodeQL, Semgrep, ESLint, GitHub code scanning, Azure DevOps, and many others. GitHub Advanced Security accepts SARIF for third-party scan ingestion. A data format; the value-add is in the tools that produce/consume SARIF, not in the format itself — placed under reference per the methodology-vs-reference distinction (see DECISIONS.md ADR-009 when codified).

examples.note: The SARIF 2.1.0 specification as a whole

data.description: Profile 5 of CSAF 2.0, defining a structured format for vendor statements about whether a product is affected by a vulnerability. The four canonical statuses are: not_affected, affected, fixed, under_investigation.
data.notes: VEX is a concept implemented by multiple specs: the CSAF VEX profile (this entry, an OASIS standard) and OpenVEX (separate spec at openvex.dev). Use the CSAF VEX entry when the source is publishing VEX statements as part of their CSAF advisory pipeline; use the OpenVEX entry (secid:reference/openvex.dev/openvex) for sources using the Chainguard/Linux-Foundation-aligned alternative. Both follow the same conceptual model.
